solution.p: We will factor 49r^{2}+14r+1 where a=49, b=14, and c=1.

Perfect Square Formula: The formula for perfect square trinomials is (a \pm b)^2=a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2. Note that the polynomial 49r^{2}+14r+1 matches the formula's right side, and we have: \begin{aligned}[t]49r^{2}+14r+1\amp =\left(7r\right)^2+2\left(7r\right)\left(1\right)+\left(1\right)^2\newline \amp =\left(7r+1\right)\mathopen{}\left(7r+1\right)\newline \amp =\left(7r+1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (49) \cdot (1) = 49.

The number 49 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(49)\amp -1(-49)\amp 7(7)\amp -7(-7)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 14, and this sum happens with 7+7=14 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]49r^{2}+14r+1\amp =49r^{2}+7r+7r+1
ewline \amp =7r\mathopen{}\left(7r+1ight)+7r+1
ewline \amp =\left(7r+1ight)\mathopen{}\left(7r+1ight)
ewline \amp =\left(7r+1ight)^{2}\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in 49r^{2}+14r+1 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 49, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(49)\amp 7(7)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp -1(-1)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with 49r^{2}+14r+1 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:

Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]49r^{2}+14r+1\amp =\left(7r+1\right)\mathopen{}\left(7r+1\right)\newline \amp =\left(7r+1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
